blocks.text: For you know what instructions we gave you through the Lord Jesus.
For this is the will of God, your sanctification: that you abstain from fornication;
that each one of you know how to control your own body in holiness and honor,

4 : is only one of three ways to translate the metaphoric use of the Gk “skeuos,” lit. “vessel.” Here it could refer to a man's wife or his penis. In any case, the general sense is that the Thessalonians preserve sexual decorum in dealing with one another.
not with lustful passion, like the Gentiles who do not know God;
that no one wrong or exploit a brother or sister in this matter, because the Lord is an avenger in all these things, just as we have already told you beforehand and solemnly warned you.
For God did not call us to impurity but in holiness.
Therefore whoever rejects this rejects not human authority but God, who also gives his Holy Spirit to you.
4.1–8: Proper sexual conduct.
Now concerning love of the brothers and sisters,a you do not need to have anyone write to you, for you yourselves have been taught by God to love one another;
9 : The basis for affirming their brotherly and sisterly love (Gk “philadelphia”) rests in their being “God-taught,” a term coined by the writers.
and indeed you do love all the brothers and sistersa throughout Macedonia. But we urge you, beloved,a to do so more and more,
to aspire to live quietly, to mind your own affairs, and to work with your hands, as we directed you,
11 : suggests that the Thessalonians are manual laborers like Paul and his companions (see. 2.9n.).
so that you may behave properly toward outsiders and be dependent on no one.
4.9–12: Care for one another.
11–12 : The injunction to and indicates that unlike many other groups at that time, the Thessalonians are not to rely on patrons to fund their activities.
But we do not want you to be uninformed, brothers and sisters,a about those who have died, so that you may not grieve as others do who have no hope.
13 : , lit., “fallen asleep,” suggesting a nonpermanent state.
For since we believe that Jesus died and rose again, even so, through Jesus, God will bring with him those who have died.
For this we declare to you by the word of the Lord, that we who are alive, who are left until the coming of the Lord, will by no means precede those who have died.a

15 : might refer to a tradition of a teaching of Jesus or Paul may be indicating this was revealed to him by the risen Jesus. , also v. 17; the writers anticipate that these events will occur within their own lifetime.
For the Lord himself, with a cry of command, with the archangel's call and with the sound of God's trumpet, will descend from heaven, and the dead in Christ will rise first.
16 : , , and are associated with the triumphal entrance of an emperor into a city.
Then we who are alive, who are left, will be caught up in the clouds together with them to meet the Lord in the air; and so we will be with the Lord forever.
17 : When the dead and living are their communal identity is reforged; separation is only temporary.
Therefore encourage one another with these words.

4.13–18: The fate of the dead and Jesus's return. A message of consolation for the Thessalonians, who seem distraught that some members have died before the return of Jesus. The imagery is similar to that found in 1 Cor 15.51–53, and is developed from Jewish apocalyptic thought.
18 : The purpose of this brief description is not to be predictive of the last days but to the Thessalonians.
